concept_name: Why Electric Current Is Not a Vector
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 5.1 — the canonical 'scalar with direction' trap; split from scalar_vs_vector bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: A home electrical switchboard where two supply wires merge into one. The total current arriving at the switch is the arithmetic sum of the two incoming currents — it does not depend on the angle at which the wires approach each other.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A river delta where two streams merge. The total water flowing downstream is the sum of the two upstream flows — the angle at which they join does not change the total.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Two taps filling the same bucket from different directions. The water level rises at the combined rate regardless of the angle between the taps. Compare this with two people pushing a trolley from different angles — there the net push does depend on the angle.

formulas.kirchhoff: i_total = i1 + i2  at any junction, any angle
formulas.wrong_vector_prediction: |i_vec| = sqrt(i1^2 + i2^2 + 2 i1 i2 cos theta)  — what current would obey IF it were a vector, but does not

constraints: Total current at a junction is always the scalar sum — Kirchhoff's junction rule
Vector prediction only equals scalar sum at theta = 0. For any other angle, the two diverge — and measurement always sides with the scalar sum.
This is the definitive reason current is classified as a scalar despite having a direction of flow
Current density J IS a vector — but current i through a cross-section is not

STATE_1.title: Current Has Direction. So Is It a Vector?

scene_composition.text:
Current flows FROM -> TO.
It has a direction.

So - is it a vector
like velocity or force?

tts_sentences.text_en: Look at this wire junction. Two wires meet at point O, each carrying a current.
i one is three amperes flowing in. i two is four amperes flowing in.
Current clearly has a direction — it flows from one point to another.
By that logic, shouldn't current be a vector, just like velocity or force?
Most students answer yes. The next three states will show exactly why that answer is wrong.

STATE_2.title: Vary the Angle. Reality Stays. Vector Prediction Wanders.

scene_composition.text:
Measured at junction
i = i1 + i2 = 7 A

Constant for every theta.

IF current were a vector
|i| = sqrt(i1^2 + i2^2 + 2 i1 i2 cos theta)
theta =   0  ->  7.00 A
theta =  60  ->  6.08 A
theta = 120  ->  3.61 A
theta = 180  ->  1.00 A

tts_sentences.text_en: Now the test. Let the angle between the two wires sweep from zero degrees to one hundred eighty.
At every angle, we compare two numbers.
Reality — what you actually measure at the junction — is always seven amperes. Three plus four. The angle does not enter the calculation.
Vector prediction — what current would obey if it were a vector — falls from seven down to one as the angle opens up.
At sixty degrees it predicts six point zero eight amperes. Reality says seven. The prediction misses by nearly a full ampere.
The two numbers agree only when the wires are parallel. For every other angle, they disagree. That is the failure of the vector model.

STATE_3.title: Kirchhoff's Junction Rule — The Formal Proof

scene_composition.text:
Kirchhoff's junction rule
(charge conservation)

Charge in = Charge out
=> i1 + i2 = i

NO angle term.
NO cosine.
Pure scalar arithmetic.

tts_sentences.text_en: The formal reason is charge conservation. Whatever charge enters the junction per second must leave it per second.
Three amperes in plus four amperes in gives seven amperes out. This is Kirchhoff's junction rule.
Notice the equation. i one plus i two equals i. No angle. No cosine. Just plain scalar addition.
Vectors cannot behave this way. When you add two vectors, the angle between them always enters through a cosine.
Current has a direction of flow, but it adds like a scalar. That is why physics classifies it as a scalar despite the direction.

scene_composition.text:
JEE Assertion-Reason

Assertion (A):  Current is a vector.
Reason (R):     Current has a direction of flow.

Verdict

A is FALSE.
R is TRUE.

Why?  Direction is necessary but
not sufficient. The parallelogram
law must also hold — it does not
for current.

Correct choice:
(A is false, R is true)

tts_sentences.text_en: This is the JEE trap you will see in assertion-reason format.
Assertion: current is a vector. Reason: current has a direction of flow.
Students who stop at the reason tick both true and pick the wrong option.
The reason is true — current does have a direction. But the assertion is false.
Having direction is only one of the two conditions for a vector. The second condition is parallelogram addition, and current fails it.
The correct answer is: assertion false, reason true.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Any quantity with a direction is a vector
trigger_phrases: current has direction so it is a vector
anything with direction is a vector
current must be a vector it flows one way
why is current not a vector even though it has direction

STATE_1.title: Your Belief — Current Direction Must Make It a Vector

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

Current has direction.
Vectors have direction.
=> current is a vector.

We'll test this.

tts_sentences.text_en: Your reasoning is natural. Current flows from one end of a wire to the other — it plainly has a direction.
And everything that has a direction — velocity, force, displacement — is a vector. So current should also be a vector.
The logic looks airtight. But it has one gap. Having a direction is the first condition for being a vector. There is a second condition, and that is where current fails.
Let me show you the test that separates true vectors from scalars that merely carry direction.

STATE_2.title: The Parallelogram Test — What Real Vectors Do

scene_composition.text:
Parallelogram law
|R|^2 = |A|^2 + |B|^2 + 2|A||B| cos theta

At 90 degrees, cos = 0
=> R = sqrt(9 + 16) = 5

Displacement obeys this.
Does current?

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is a real vector at work. Walk three metres east, then four metres north.
Your final displacement is not seven metres. It is five metres at fifty-three degrees north of east.
Displacement obeys the parallelogram law — the resultant magnitude depends on the angle between the two legs.
That is the second condition for being a vector. Real vectors add through the parallelogram law, and the angle matters.
Now we check whether current passes this test.

scene_composition.text:
Vector prediction (wrong)
|i| = sqrt(9 + 16 + 24 cos 60)
    = sqrt(37) ~ 6.08 A

What the ammeter reads
i = i1 + i2
  = 3 + 4
  = 7 A  (always)

tts_sentences.text_en: Apply the parallelogram law to the currents at sixty degrees.
The vector model predicts six point zero eight amperes.
Put an ammeter on the outgoing wire and measure. You read seven amperes. Always. Every time.
The two numbers disagree, and measurement wins. The vector model is wrong for current.
Current carries a direction but does not obey the second condition. It fails the parallelogram law. That is why it is a scalar.

scene_composition.text:
Vector requires BOTH

(1)  Has magnitude AND direction
(2)  Obeys parallelogram law of addition

Current satisfies (1).
Current fails (2).

=> Current is a scalar.

Other traps that fool
your original rule

pressure           scalar
surface tension    scalar
finite rotation    not a vector

All have direction.
All fail condition (2).

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the rule written cleanly. A vector needs both conditions, not just the first.
Condition one — magnitude and direction. Condition two — obey the parallelogram law of addition.
Current passes one and fails two, so it is a scalar.
The same trap catches pressure, surface tension, and finite rotation. All of them have directional features, none of them obey the parallelogram law, so none of them are vectors.
Next time you see a quantity with direction, do not stop at condition one. Test condition two.

regeneration_variants.label: Different currents, same junction logic
regeneration_variants.physical_world: i1 = 5 A, i2 = 12 A, any angle. Measured total always 17 A. Vector prediction drops from 17 (parallel) to 7 (anti-parallel).
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Kirchhoff junction rule: i = i1 + i2, angle-independent

regeneration_variants.label: Three-wire junction (harder)
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Three wires bringing i1, i2, i3 into a junction. Measured outgoing current is i1 + i2 + i3 regardless of the three angles. Same scalar-addition behaviour, now in 3-way form.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Kirchhoff junction rule scales to any number of incoming wires — always scalar sum
